questions.question: What does it mean for a build to be 'hermetic'?
options: Its output is a function only of declared inputs — not of the developer's machine state
It runs in a sealed virtual machine
questions.explanation: Hermeticity is about *correctness*: same inputs give the same output bytes everywhere. Bazel sandboxes each action so it only sees declared inputs; Make is non-hermetic by default because recipes can read anything in scope.

questions.question: What does CMake actually do?
options: Generates a real build file (Makefile, Ninja, MSBuild) from CMakeLists.txt, then you invoke that builder to build
questions.explanation: CMake is a *generator*: `cmake -S . -B build -G Ninja` reads CMakeLists.txt and writes build.ninja into build/. Then `cmake --build build` or `ninja -C build` actually runs the build. CMake itself doesn't compile anything.

questions.question: Bazel decides a build action is up to date by checking what?
options: Content hashes of declared inputs + the exact command line + tool version
Whether the source compiled cleanly last time
questions.explanation: Bazel uses a content-addressed cache keyed on input hashes (and the exact command + toolchain). This is what makes the cache shareable across machines: two developers with the same inputs hit the same cache entry. mtime-based systems can't safely share caches because timestamps differ per checkout.

questions.question: A Make-based project edits a header file. Without `-MMD -MP`, what happens on the next `make`?
options: Only files that include the header rebuild
Nothing rebuilds; Make sees no changed mtimes among declared prereqs
questions.explanation: Make only knows about prereqs you list. If a `.h` file isn't a listed prereq of a `.o` rule, editing it changes nothing Make can see. Result: stale binaries. `-MMD -MP` makes the compiler emit dep files listing every header each `.c` actually included; Make includes those `.d` files to learn the real graph.

questions.question: Your team has a Rust binary, a Go service, and a Python tool, all sharing a protobuf schema and shipping in one container. What's the best build system?
options: Stick with Cargo, treat the others as external
Bazel (or Buck2) — its strength is polyglot, hermetic, cache-shared builds across an org
Make at the top level orchestrating the three
questions.explanation: Polyglot monorepo with shared schema = Bazel's sweet spot. One BUILD file per language, hermetic actions, shared remote cache, distributed remote execution. Cargo/Make/CI-only approaches all leave the cross-language dependency graph implicit; Bazel makes it explicit and verifiable.

questions.question: Cargo decides a target is fresh by checking what?
options: A fingerprint over source files + Cargo.lock + features + rustc version + relevant flags
questions.explanation: Cargo's incremental check is fingerprint-based, not mtime-based: it hashes the inputs (source, lockfile, features, rustc version, flags) and compares to the previous fingerprint stored in `target/`. This is why Cargo handles toolchain upgrades correctly: bump rustc → fingerprint changes → everything rebuilds.

_meta.title: Build Systems Make Cmake Bazel Cargo
